speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had made the right decision again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This was a critical decision, of course, because it was so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] momentous to, to, to consider that all of the ones that returned with the safety of the Russian Army thought they would certainly be OK and were arrested by the NKVD and never heard from again. Clearly, that would have been our fate. Because when they were sent to Siberia, it wasn't just them-- not the menfolk. It was the whole family. And so we, too, would have ended up somewhere in Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a signal for you for the rest of your life, to be wary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure. I'm sure all of this played itself out the rest of my life, in terms of fear and, and what I began to, to, to understand, my mother's so-called Bialystok Syndrome, that danger lurks around every corner. I, I guess I lived my life having that feeling transmitted by virtue of these events and my mother's feeling. And I guess it served me in many ways, because I always thought ahead as a consequence. And, you know, when I'm accredited in, in the marketplace with having vision, I think that vision was based on this fear factor. You've gotta think ahead to survive, which is true in business as well as in ordinary life. And I think that certainly was the beginning of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anticipate the worst-case scenario always?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have to. Well, it's the what-if scenarios. Obviously, it's the, it's the similar thing that the Pentagon does in its war room, you know, eventualities and what-if situations and the worst kind, the best kind, and so forth. You prepare. And in my mind, that was always going on as an adult. And I think it clearly has its origin in these events of the early days of my life and in Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father had done the right thing, once again and didn't return. But we didn't know where he was. And when the NKVD came, they said as soon as we hear from him, be sure to tell them. You know, my mother agreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, she didn't know how she would hear from him. Because you must remember, we didn't have a telephone. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] eventually-- I mean, maybe semaphore, but we didn't know how. And it was a worrisome time. Then suddenly one evening Mrs. London-- that was her name, a friend who lived nearby-- came rushing to the house. She had a telephone in her house, because they, they had a store. And my father was on the phone. And my mother left with her, rushing to answer the phone call and returned an hour or so later, having talked with my father. And she told me that he was OK and that he had learned that Stalin was gonna give Vilna over to the Lithuanians. Vilna had always been on the cusp between Lithuania and Poland. And there was a tug-of-war, always, about Vilna, the city, whether it was really Lithuanian or whether it was really Polish. And Stalin, in his magnanimity, was giving Vilna up, once again, to the Lithuanians, as it turned out, only for a little while. But nevertheless, that meant that Vilna would fall under the Lithuanian rule and not Russian rule. And my father felt safer. Therefore it was agreed between him and my mother that we would take, that evening, the train out of Bialystok, because the border would be closed the following morning when this eventuality happened. And if we didn't catch this last train out of Bialystok to Vilna, we wouldn't, at least in a conventional way, be able to go to Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we packed, said our goodbyes. Everybody came with us to the [NON-ENGLISH], the, the, the train station, and we left for Vilna. Again, a memory that I will never forget, that scene at the train station with what seemed like thousands of people rushing about, trying to get on trains. Well, whether they all knew what my father knew or not, there were people running from and running to, and it was total mayhem and confusion and, and chaos.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got on board a train. I, because of my age, was given a window seat. This was a most coveted occasion-- vantage point, to be able to sit at the window because people were sitting on boxes and baggage and, and, and standing. Usually the, the the train ride to Vilna, in conventional time, was a couple hours, at most. This took all night, because the train would stop in the middle of the night. We would hear shouts. Sometimes we would hear gunfire. People with voices could be heard in the distance sometimes. So it was very eerie and scary. I would always awake during those intervals, because once the train started to roll again, I would nod off to sleep, of course, but would be awakened the very next time it stopped. And it stopped more than it went, because it took so long to get to Vilna. We arrived in the morning, actually. It took all night. And I spotted my father-- because I remember he was in a [NON-ENGLISH], a hat. And our eyes met for, for a fraction of a second. He saw us through the window, and I saw him. And then the train moved on, and we lost each other in a sea of people but eventually hooked up with him again. And of course there was a joyous reunion. My mother had not packed a lot, because we didn't know we were leaving for good. We were leaving for an interim. My mother said to me later that maybe she would have never left if she thought that she was leaving my grandmother-- her mother-- and my aunt and my other grandmother forever. This was not the parting it was, that night we left. It was for interim. It was for a day or two, a week, a month. Nothing serious. Didn't turn out that way, of course. But we arrived in Bialystok, and my father had rented a one-room flat overlooking [? Strashun ?] Street. That was the Jewish quarter in Vilna. And it was the butchers' street. It was the street on which all the butcher shops of the world, I thought, existed, where you could, you could buy anything that related to a, to a cow or an oxen or to a chicken or to a geese-- a goose or what have you. And the, the street was laden with meats of every kind, butcher shops of every kind. In fact, the, the gutter, often as not, was red from blood of the, of the killing of the animals involved and the, the wielding of the butcher knives and so forth as they cut off, for their customers that came to, to buy meat, slices of beef or slices of turkeys or goose. And the, the, the people would come to buy. They'd come with their [NON-ENGLISH], the, the handbags and the bags. And they would go to the shops and buy and stand in line to get their food. And the, the street was always bustling, except by Friday night, when things would get-- be very quiet. This was the Jewish quarter, and of course Friday night was the beginning of the Sabbath. And everything quieted down, and all the shops closed. And Saturday it was very, very quiet. Sunday, too, and it didn't start until Monday again. Our room that was rented overlooked this very scene. And I would often stand in front of the little window, which had a little grating where you could step out, actually, onto a veranda, a very small veranda, just enough for one person. And I would, for hours, stand there and watch, in awe, this scene below me, truly mesmerized by what I saw and the people haggling and arguing and buying and selling and-- you know, a scene that, literally, it does not exist anymore, as far as I know, anywhere in the world. This was unusual. Very colorful. Extremely interesting. Highly unusual.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what gave you your taste for buying and selling?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I don't think so. I, I, I, I was just overwhelmed by the scene. It was just out of science fiction. It may have given me a little bit of that. But, really, truly, a marvel, especially for a young kid.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of a sudden, you know, things were a little bit more normal. My father and mother were around. And I was, I was enrolled in school-- now first grade.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I met my first real girlfriend. Her name was Esther, [NON-ENGLISH]. Her last name was Mann. And the-- her parents were teachers. And my parents knew them from the Vilna seminary. So this was like a reunion for them. They lived nearby, and Esther took me in. She was my age. She-- we started first grade together, and we became inseparable. If it wasn't for her, I don't know how I would have existed, because I was fairly shy. In fact, I was more comfortable among adults than I was among children. I hadn't had that much with children. I was-- I lived a lot, in my mind, alone, a lot of this period of time. And adults were normal. Children weren't. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So she took me in, and it was truly my first girlfriend. And [SIGHS] we spent all our time in school and after school together. The, the premises had a inner courtyard where many of the buildings opened up into so that the courtyard became a haven for children to play games, to walk around, to sit and talk, and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in that courtyard I learned all the facts of life and everything that you would think-- you could think of that kids talk about or, or learn because there were many ages in that courtyard. There were kids-- five-year-olds and four-year-olds, as well as 16- and 17-year-olds-- mingling together. And so you get to pick up everything. And I think I really became quite an adult-- even though I was only eight years old-- in that courtyard. But life was fairly normal for the next-- normal in the sense that we were no longer being plagued by-- or there wasn't that fear factor around. There was nobody bombing you. But-- and my parents got jobs as teachers. So in this respect I was normal. It lasted for about six months. And suddenly things changed and this time for good, because Stalin and the Russians decided to take over not just Vilna, but all of Lithuania, declaring war or whatever. [SIGHS] At that time I didn't know what it was. It was explained again to my father. And this meant, now, that my father would become, again, a refugee, because he would have to run and leave us. The reason was obvious. He had correctly fled-- not returned to the Russians. Certainly he wasn't going to fall into their trap and be found here in Vilna, because the same fate would await him. So he left. And we were again-- the fear factor returned, because now we were always afraid that someone would come searching for him. And my mother would use a signal. She would hang a, a, a towel over the rail in, in the window in the veranda. If the towel was hanging there, that meant that my father could come and visit us. If the towel wasn't there, that meant that it was dangerous for him to come. And we lived in this fashion, with fear, for another-- nearly a year. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was at this point that I later learned that my father applied for a visa to Japan. Because by getting a visa, it meant you could ask for permission to leave Russia, leave Russian rule. If you didn't have any place to go, then you would not be able to apply. But if you had a visa, you could then apply. That's the issue of Sugihara, the Polish-- the-- sorry, the Japanese ambassador to Lithuania that gave out thousands of visas to Jews and saved their lives by doing so, because they were able to leave Lithuania-- Russia. And if they didn't, they would have been caught in the trap, because it wasn't long after that that the Germans captured Lithuania. And all of the Jewish community, which was a thriving-- hundreds of thousands of Jews were exterminated. There were only 10,000 Lithuanian Jews left in all of Lithuania, never mind Vilna, after the war. And so that visa saved the lives of many thousands of Jews, among them the Melamdoviches.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the, the visa that said you could stop in Japan on your way to Curacao? [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was later. I'm not sure whether you had to identify-- I, I suppose that was a fictitious place that you had to say. Because it was only a transit visa, that's right. So it, it, it identified, for most people, that they were on their way to Curacao. But you didn't have to be on your way to Curacao. You could be on your way to anywhere. The visa was transit, and the idea was to get into Japan, from which you would leave. Nobody really wanted to stay in Japan, anyway. This was foreign territory of a real different nature that most of the refugees didn't want to remain in. My father applied along with, as I said, thousands of others. Fortunately, we got the visa. And then, of course, there was this huge danger, that by applying for permission to leave with the visa, he had to identify himself. And he had to identify himself as Isaac Moishe Melamdovich. If it was connected by whoever was the Russian administrator in, in, in, in granting permission to leave that we were really the Melamdoviches that were wanted in Bialystok, then, of course, the fate would have been Siberia, at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a big danger in making that decision, but-- the decision which I partook in, as if my parents cared of my opinion. But they told me that the decision would have to be made whether to apply-- now that he got his visa to Japan-- or not and that the danger was that if they connected it up to who we really were that the chances were very good that we would be arrested and gone, sent to Siberia. How did you vote?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I voted to go, because that's what my parents told me they were voting. So I voted with them, and it became a unanimous vote to apply. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: During the time in, in Lithuania, was your father still writing for [DRINKS] Bundist outlets, or was he laying low, more or less?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was laying low. By then there was very little writing. It was mostly surviving. And he was a, he was a, a survivor, with others, in the forests surrounding Lithuania or surrounding Kaunas and would occasionally go into Kaunas. That's how he learned about the Japanese foreign ministry there and the fact that the Japanese [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ambassador, Sugihara, could grant visas to those who applied.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got to Vilna when?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got to Vilna it was in September of 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because we were under German rule only for a couple of weeks. So it had to be towards the end of September that we got to Vilna. And we stayed there from 1941 September-- 1939, sorry, September, through late 1940. By that, I mean probably December, when we left for Moscow. So it was over a year that we remained in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the visas were issued in August?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were issued in August of 1940. But we had to then apply for permission to leave. The visa in itself didn't get you out. The visa only was the ticket to get out. You then had to apply to the Russian foreign ministry to leave and grant permission to leave. And my father still didn't come back to Vilna during that period of time. My mother and I would go, as a routine, on a Friday afternoon, to see if our name was listed among those who were granted permission to leave. A list would be [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] posted for those who were granted permission.

speaker: interviewer
text: Posted where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Posted at the Russian Ministry. So we would go there. It wasn't really the Russian Ministry. It was just taken over by the Russians. It was previously the Polish Ministry and then the Lithuanian Ministry and now the Russian Ministry. In fact, that's a little interesting anecdote that I ought to mention, is the fact that in my first grade, in school, I began a, a, a kind of a journey of language that lasted a long, long time. You must remember that my two early languages were Yiddish and Polish. When I got to, to Vilna and it became Lithuanian, the school was Lithuanian. And Lithuanian became the language that we were being taught. So this was my third language. When the Russians took over, the same school-- I was still in first grade, or I was on my way to second, I think-- and the, and the language now became Russian. This was my fourth language. Eventually it was-- by the time I was in-- eligible for third grade I began to learn Japanese. And by the time I got to the United States-- this whole period of time, as we're talking, is a, is a, a two-year period-- I was learning English. So we're talking about a real confused kid with lots of languages, something like six. Anyway-- I didn't learn them all, but I learned a little bit. And so my father, in, in Vilna, was still living away from us, living undercover. Because he was afraid that he would be caught and that two and two would be added and who he really was-- a prominent anti-communist-- would be discovered. But lo and behold, a few months after we've applied, there was our name, granting us permission to leave. And we hurriedly made the preparations, bought the tickets. The tickets would require that we go to Moscow, because in Moscow we could catch the trans-Siberian train that would take us to Vladivostok, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the border of the Japanese ocean and there-- from there be transported by boat to Japan. So that began in December of 1940. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an overnight trip to Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your girlfriend's family come, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Only we left, because we had the visa. And they were, of course, permanent citizens of, of Vilna, without any intention of leaving. You know, to leave where you live, you had to have a desperate motivation. No one knew what was in store for those who didn't leave. No one could imagine the atrocities that were forthcoming. No one could imagine the horror of the war that was going to happen. It was much more likely that you could survive better staying in, in your house in, in, in the surroundings you knew best with the language and people that could protect you better than to go off into the unknown, where no one was certain of a destiny at all if you could survive the travels and the dangers of travel during a time of war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So those that were running were really a small percentage of the population. And in our case, we were already refugees. We were already running. We had a visa where we could go. So it was kind of a psychological ease for us to keep moving and my father's motivation to keep moving.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, again, one of those critical changes in our destiny, without which we wouldn't made it out. As I said, the population of Vilna was destroyed. The Jewish population was exterminated. The Manns were killed. My grandmothers and, and, and my aunt was burned to death in the, in the Bialystok synagogue, the, the grosse shul. But none of that was known. None of that could be predicted. No one could predict this. After all, wars had happened before, people survived during the war, populations exist. World War I, these people had all remained where they were during World War I. And so they-- it was not something unknown. A war is a war, but the populations can survive a war. No one expected the horror of World War II as it unfolded. My father had made the critically right decision to run, and we were one of the few families that got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a cold winter day when you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we left from Vilna, no. When we left from Moscow, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As I recall, Moscow was an awesome, awesome place that left me memories forever-- the spires, the domes, the red wall of the Kremlin, imposing things. We lived in, in a hotel. By then we were under the direction of, of the Intourist bureau, because we were leaving Russia. Anyone that leaves Russia is treated very well, so you'd have good memories of Russia, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were allowed to visit and be tourists, as if we were tourists in Moscow. And I remember that. It was a vivid memory in-- of mine, because it was so thrilling, I mean, absolutely thrilling, the things I saw.

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw Lenin in his tomb, embalmed in his tomb, sitting as if he was lifelike and this soldier standing in erect attention around this-- the mausoleum that he, he lie in. And, and we, we came to, to look at him, as tourists. I'll never forget that scene. I don't know-- God knows where Lenin is today. But then he was-- you know, it was a place of honor, extreme honor. And we saw that. So all of this was thrilling. The day we left for Moscow-- we were in Moscow three days. And my father said he never, never-- the feeling that someone was following him never left his side, even though we were seeing all these sights and touring. And, and, in fact, that feeling was transmitted to me, that all was not quite as the surface would seem. And I'm sure he was right, that people followed us, and, and we were watched and so forth. But the day we left was very cold. And Moscow can get quite cold. And this was December. And, in fact, it was into the-- probably the second week of December. And I know that because we celebrated New Year's-- or at least I have a memory of that-- on the train. So it must have been just before we hit Vladivostok that we welcomed January 1, 1941 on the train in the trans-Siberian railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, the next episode was my memories of that train ride, which, of course, stand out in my mind and will forever. Because, I mean, how many people spend two weeks on a train, literally two weeks on a train? It's a train ride that's supposed to take about nine or 10 days, but it took two weeks. It's a single line from Moscow to Vladivostok. It was a single line so that you would have layovers in big cities for the southbound train to pass you by.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We, we, of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would spend countless hours [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sitting around or watching through the window or-- what the older folk did, the men's folk, certainly-- play chess. And I learned to play chess by watching them play. Much to my father's surprise, by the time we hit Japan, I could play chess, which he didn't realize happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you beat him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. [LAUGHS] He was a very good chess player. It wasn't until many years later that I could play as well as he could. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- the things I did on that train was watch out the window or watch chess. That was about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a suspension of fear, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except that there was always this constant unknown that I knew my parents had, that at any time, at any place, someone could connect up who my father was and arrest us.
